summary: Officially documented control for Win32 long paths. Good for development and tooling workflows that hit the 260-character path limit, but it only helps applications that opt in to long-path support.

setting.name: Win32 Long Paths
setting.casual_explanation: Windows normally keeps many apps limited to shorter file paths. This setting allows long paths for apps that are built to support them.
setting.why_people_change_it: People enable it when development tools, package managers, archives, or deep folder structures hit the normal path-length limit.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning it on helps long-path-aware apps, but older apps that do not opt in still keep their normal limitations.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents LongPathsEnabled under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\FileSystem in the maximum path limitation guidance. FileSys.admx maps the same path and value name with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0. FileSys.adml says enabling the setting allows manifested Win32 applications and packaged Microsoft Store applications to access paths beyond the normal 260-character limit.

allowed_values.label: Default behavior
allowed_values.meaning: Win32 long paths are not explicitly enabled through this registry value.

allowed_values.label: Long paths disabled
allowed_values.meaning: Win32 long paths are not enabled through this setting.

allowed_values.label: Long paths enabled
allowed_values.meaning: Long-path-aware applications can access paths beyond the normal 260-character limit.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes LongPathsEnabled = 1 on the Microsoft-documented path.

windows_defaults.label: Windows default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 10 and later systems that have not explicitly enabled long paths

states.rationale: Leave the value unset unless long-path support is needed.

windows_defaults.notes: Microsoft documents the registry value as the enablement mechanism. When it is not set, long-path support is not explicitly enabled for Win32 apps through this control.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems that do not hit path-length limits
avoid_for: Developer workstations that regularly hit deep path trees

states.rationale: Leave the long-path setting at its normal baseline.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You do not have applications or workflows that hit the path-length limit.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You regularly hit long path errors in development or packaging workflows.
tradeoffs: Windows keeps its normal path-limit baseline.
Long-path-aware apps do not gain the extended path behavior through this setting.

recommended_profiles.label: Long-path aware workflows
intended_for: Systems using deep folder trees or package caches
avoid_for: Machines that do not need extended path support

states.rationale: Enable Win32 long paths for apps that opt in.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You hit long-path issues in developer tools or file-heavy workflows.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You do not need the feature and want to keep the system closer to its default baseline.
tradeoffs: Long-path-aware applications can exceed the old 260-character limit.
Older apps that do not opt in still keep their normal limitations.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Maximum Path Length Limitation
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents LongPathsEnabled under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\FileSystem and explains that a value of 1 enables long paths for applications that opt in.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft FileSys.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps LongPathsEnabled to System\CurrentControlSet\Control\FileSystem\LongPathsEnabled with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft FileSys.adml help text
evidence.summary: The local ADML help text says enabling the setting allows manifested Win32 applications and packaged Microsoft Store applications to access paths beyond the normal 260-character limit.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes LongPathsEnabled = 1 for the current tweak.

decision.why: Local Microsoft FileSys policy files document the exact machine registry surface and the long-path behavior. The app writes the documented enabled value.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: <policy name="LongPathsEnabled" class="Machine" ... key="System\CurrentControlSet\Control\FileSystem" valueName="LongPathsEnabled">; FileSys.adml: Enabling Win32 long paths will allow manifested win32 applications and packaged Microsoft Store applications to access paths beyond the normal 260 character limit.
validation_proof.notes: Local official ADMX defines the exact machine key and value name; local ADML confirms that enabling the policy allows long-path-aware Win32 and packaged apps to access paths beyond the normal 260-character limit.
